questions.prompt: Your user said 'I'm out of office this week' once. Two months later the assistant still signs off 'enjoy your time off.' What's the underlying issue?

choices.text: Working memory has no expiration on temporal preferences. Time-bound facts (out of office, project deadlines, this-week-only contexts) need automatic expiration (1-2 weeks default) or explicit confirmation to persist. Without aging, working memory drifts from current reality.

questions.explanation: Working memory ages. Temporal preferences (out of office, vacation, this-quarter goals) should expire quickly. Durable identity preferences (pronouns, communication style) can promote to long-term memory. The line is type, not just age — but anything that smells time-bound should default to short TTL.

questions.prompt: What's the canonical 'welcome back' UX powered by working memory?

choices.text: 'Welcome back. Want to continue: [active task 1], [active task 2], or something new?' — surfacing in-flight work as continuation options. Loaded from working memory at session start. Users feel oriented immediately; reduces re-explaining what they were working on.
Show all memory at the start.
Ask the user what they want.

questions.explanation: Continuation prompts powered by working memory are the highest-value pattern for session-to-session continuity. They make the assistant feel like a coworker who remembers what you were doing. Include user controls to hide/edit so they stay welcome and not invasive.
